“Validation Belongs on Both Sides: Angular Forms and Spring Boot APIs” is not just a slogan. It’s a response to a real problem most teams hit: your Angular form looks solid, then the Spring Boot API rejects the request in production, or worse, accepts invalid data that breaks reporting, search, or downstream logic.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

In real applications, frontend validation is about fast feedback and fewer pointless requests. Backend validation is about protecting your system when the client is buggy, outdated, automated, or malicious. If you do only one side, you will either frustrate users or corrupt data.
Why validation belongs on both sides (and what goes wrong if it doesn’t)
This usually looks harmless at the beginning: you add a couple of Angular validators, disable the submit button, and call it done. Later, you add a mobile app, an import script, or a second UI. Or someone bypasses the UI with Postman. Now your database becomes the validator, which is the worst time to discover missing rules.
Common failure modes I see:

• Rules drift: Angular allows values that Spring Boot rejects (or the reverse).

• Unhelpful errors: backend returns a generic 400 and the UI can’t…
